input.command:
cat > README.md <<'EOF'
# Spring Boot + OpenAI GPT Demo

This is a simple Spring Boot project that calls OpenAI GPT through a REST API.

## Requirements

- Java 17+
- Maven 3.9+
- OpenAI API key

## Configuration

Set your API key as an environment variable:

```bash
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your_openai_api_key"
```

On Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
$env:OPENAI_API_KEY="your_openai_api_key"
```

Default model is set to `gpt-4o-mini` in `src/main/resources/application.yml`.

## Run

```bash
mvn spring-boot:run
```

## API Usage

POST request:

```http
POST /api/chat
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "prompt": "Explain Spring Boot in one paragraph"
}
```

Example curl:

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"prompt":"Write a haiku about Java"}'
```

Response example:

```json
{
  "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
  "reply": "..."
}
```
EOF

         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 https://maven.apache.org/xsd/maven-4.0.0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>

    <description>Library Management System API</description>

        <java.version>17</java.version>
        <jjwt.version>0.12.6</jjwt.version>
    </properties>

    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>

        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>

        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-validation</artifactId>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-actuator</artifactId>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.mysql</groupId>
            <artifactId>mysql-connector-j</artifactId>
            <scope>runtime</scope>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.jsonwebtoken</groupId>
            <artifactId>jjwt-api</artifactId>
            <version>${jjwt.version}</version>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.jsonwebtoken</groupId>
            <artifactId>jjwt-impl</artifactId>
            <version>${jjwt.version}</version>
            <scope>runtime</scope>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>io.jsonwebtoken</groupId>
            <artifactId>jjwt-jackson</artifactId>
            <version>${jjwt.version}</version>
            <scope>runtime</scope>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.h2database</groupId>
            <artifactId>h2</artifactId>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>

                            <goal>prepare-agent</goal>

                    <execution>
                        <id>report</id>
                        <phase>test</phase>
                        <goals>

                        </goals>
                    </execution>
                    <execution>
                        <id>check</id>
                        <phase>verify</phase>
                        <goals>
                            <goal>check</goal>

                            <rules>

                                            <value>COVEREDRATIO</value>
                                            <minimum>0.80</minimum>
                                        </limit>
                                    </limits>
                                </rule>
                            </rules>
                        </configuration>
                    </execution>
                </executions>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>

services:
  mysql:
    image: mysql:8.4
    environment:
      MYSQL_DATABASE: library_db
      MYSQL_USER: library
      MYSQL_PASSWORD: library
      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: root
    ports:
      - "3306:3306"
    volumes:
      - mysql_data:/var/lib/mysql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "mysqladmin", "ping", "-h", "localhost"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10

  backend:
    build: ./backend
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE: docker
    depends_on:
      mysql:
        condition: service_healthy

  frontend:
    build: ./frontend
    ports:
      - "5173:80"
    depends_on:
      - backend

volumes:
  mysql_data:

FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json ./
RUN npm install
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM nginx:1.27-alpine
COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
EXPOSE 80

name: Library CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, master]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, master]

jobs:
  backend:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: backend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          distribution: temurin
          java-version: 17
          cache: maven
      - name: Run tests and coverage
        run: mvn -B verify

  frontend:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:

# Library Management System

Full-stack library management application with **Spring Boot + MySQL** backend and **React** frontend.

## Features

- Secure login/signup with JWT and BCrypt password hashing
- Role-based access (`LIBRARIAN`, `MEMBER`)
- Book, author, member, and lending management
- Business rules:
  - Unique ISBN per book
  - Book issue only when available
  - Max 5 active loans per member
  - 14-day loan period
- Notifications for due/overdue books (manual + scheduled)
- API rate limiting, logging, actuator health/metrics
- Dockerized deployment and CI pipeline
- API test coverage enforced with JaCoCo (>80%)

## Project Structure

- `backend/` – Spring Boot REST API
- `frontend/` – React (Vite) responsive UI
- `docker-compose.yml` – MySQL + API + UI

<user_query>
what is the use of nginx ?
</user_query>

In this project, **Nginx** is used on the **frontend container**, not inside Spring Boot.

## What it does here

1. **Serves the React app (static files)**  
   After `npm run build`, Vite outputs HTML/JS/CSS into `dist/`. Nginx serves those files on port 80 so users get the UI in production.

2. **SPA routing**  
   In `frontend/nginx.conf`, `try_files $uri /index.html` sends unknown paths to `index.html` so React Router routes like `/books` and `/login` work on refresh and direct links.

3. **Reverse proxy to the API**  
   Browser calls go to the same host (e.g. `http://localhost:5173/api/...`). Nginx forwards them to the backend:

   - `/api/` → `http://backend:8080/api/`
   - `/actuator/` → `http://backend:8080/actuator/`

   That avoids CORS issues in Docker and keeps one entry URL for UI + API.

## Why not only React dev server?

| Environment | Tool | Role |
|-------------|------|------|
| **Development** | Vite (`npm run dev`) | Hot reload, dev proxy in `vite.config.js` |
| **Production (Docker)** | Nginx | Fast, small static server + API proxy |

Vite’s dev server is not meant for production. Nginx is a common, lightweight way to host the built React app and proxy API traffic to Spring Boot.

## Simple flow

```text
Browser → Nginx (port 80)
            ├─ /, /login, /books...  → React files from /usr/share/nginx/html
            └─ /api/*                → Spring Boot on backend:8080
```

**Short answer:** Nginx hosts your production React UI and forwards `/api` requests to the Spring Boot backend so the stack runs cleanly in Docker with a single frontend URL.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
what will happen if the nginx layer is removed ?
</user_query>

If you remove the **Nginx layer** from this project’s **Docker/production** setup, nothing magical breaks in Spring Boot or MySQL — but you lose what Nginx was doing for the frontend and routing.

## What changes

### 1. No production static host for React
The React app is just files in `dist/`. Without Nginx (or something else), you need another way to serve them, for example:

- Run `npm run preview` (not ideal for real production)
- Put the build on **S3 + CloudFront** (AWS style)
- Serve static files from Spring Boot (possible, but not how this app is set up)

If you do nothing, users won’t get the UI from the frontend container the way `docker-compose` expects.

### 2. API proxy goes away
Today, the browser can call:

`http://localhost:5173/api/auth/login`

and Nginx forwards that to `backend:8080`.

Without Nginx, the browser must call the API **directly**, e.g.:

`http://localhost:8080/api/auth/login`

So you must set `VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:8080` at build time (or change `client.js` defaults).

### 3. CORS becomes your problem
Same-origin calls (`5173` → `5173/api`) avoid extra CORS setup.  
Direct calls (`5173` → `8080`) are cross-origin; Spring Boot must allow the frontend origin in CORS (your backend already allows `http://localhost:5173`, so this can work if configured correctly).

### 4. SPA deep links may break
Nginx’s `try_files ... /index.html` makes `/books` work on refresh.  
If you serve static files without that rule (e.g. plain file server), refreshing `/books` can return **404** unless you configure the replacement host the same way.

### 5. Docker Compose layout changes
Current stack:

```text
Browser → frontend (Nginx:80) → backend:8080
```

Without Nginx, typical options:

```text
Browser → backend:8080 (API only) + separate static hosting
```

or run frontend dev server only in dev, not in compose.

## What still works

| Piece | Without Nginx |
|--------|----------------|
| Spring Boot API | Yes, on `:8080` |
| MySQL | Yes |
| `npm run dev` (Vite) | Yes — Vite replaces Nginx in **dev** (proxy in `vite.config.js`) |
| Production Docker frontend service | No — unless you replace Nginx |

## Summary

**Removing Nginx doesn’t remove a backend dependency** — it removes the **production frontend server and API gateway in front of React**. You still need:

1. Something to serve the React build  
2. A clear API base URL (`8080` or env var)  
3. CORS (if UI and API are on different ports/hosts)  
4. SPA fallback routing on whatever serves the UI  

In **local dev**, you already run without Nginx when you use `npm run dev` + `mvn spring-boot:run`. Nginx is mainly for the **containerized production-style** frontend service.

Here is a practical **AWS deployment guide** for your Library Management System (React + Spring Boot + MySQL).

---

## Target AWS layout

| Component | AWS service |
|-----------|-------------|
| React UI | **S3** + **CloudFront** |
| Spring Boot API | **ECS Fargate** + **ALB** |
| MySQL | **Amazon RDS (MySQL 8)** |
| Secrets (DB password, JWT) | **Secrets Manager** |
| Logs/metrics | **CloudWatch** |
| Container images | **ECR** |
| Optional notifications | **EventBridge** + **SNS** |

---

## Prerequisites

1. AWS account with admin (or scoped deploy) access  
2. AWS CLI configured: `aws configure`  
3. Docker installed locally  
4. Domain (optional, for HTTPS on CloudFront/ALB)  
5. Project builds locally:
   - `cd backend && mvn -DskipTests package`
   - `cd frontend && npm install && npm run build`

---

## Phase 1 — Network & database

### 1. Create VPC (or use default)

- Use **VPC** with **2 public subnets** (ALB) and **2 private subnets** (ECS + RDS) across 2 AZs (production).

### 2. Create RDS MySQL

1. **RDS** → Create database → **MySQL 8.x**  
2. Settings:
   - DB identifier: `library-db`
   - Master user: `libraryadmin`
   - Password: strong password (store in Secrets Manager)
   - DB name: `library_db`
3. Connectivity:
   - Place in **private subnet**
   - Security group: allow **3306** only from ECS task security group
4. Note endpoint: `library-db.xxxxx.region.rds.amazonaws.com`

### 3. Store secrets in Secrets Manager

Create secret `library/prod` with JSON:

```json
{
  "SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL": "jdbc:mysql://<rds-endpoint>:3306/library_db?useSSL=true&serverTimezone=UTC",
  "SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME": "libraryadmin",
  "SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD": "<password>",
  "APP_JWT_SECRET": "<long-random-secret-32+chars>"
}
```

---

## Phase 2 — Backend (ECR + ECS Fargate)

### 4. Create ECR repository

```bash
aws ecr create-repository --repository-name library-backend
```

Login and push image:

```bash
cd backend
aws ecr get-login-password --region <region> | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com

docker build -t library-backend .
docker tag library-backend:latest <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/library-backend:latest
docker push <account-id>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/library-backend:latest
```

### 5. Create ECS cluster

- **ECS** → Clusters → Create → name: `library-cluster` → **Fargate**

### 6. Task definition (backend)

Example env (or map from Secrets Manager):

- `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=docker` (or custom `prod`)
- `SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL` (from secret)
- `SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME`
- `SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD`
- `APP_JWT_SECRET`

Container:

- Image: ECR `library-backend:latest`
- Port: `8080`
- CPU/Memory: e.g. `0.5 vCPU / 1 GB` (adjust as needed)
- Log driver: `awslogs` → CloudWatch log group `/ecs/library-backend`

**IAM task role**: allow `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` for your secret.

### 7. Application Load Balancer + target group

1. Create **ALB** (internet-facing) in public subnets  
2. Target group: port **8080**, health check `/actuator/health`  
3. Listener: HTTP **80** (and HTTPS **443** with ACM certificate)

### 8. ECS service

- Launch type: **Fargate**
- Desired tasks: `2` (HA)
- Subnets: **private**
- Security group: allow inbound from ALB only on `8080`
- Attach to ALB target group

Note API URL: `http://<alb-dns-name>` (later HTTPS with ACM).

---

## Phase 3 — Frontend (S3 + CloudFront)

### 9. Build React with API URL

Set backend URL at build time:

```bash
cd frontend
# PowerShell
$env:VITE_API_URL="https://api.yourdomain.com"
npm run build
```

(`dist/` folder is your deployable UI.)

### 10. Create S3 bucket for static site

```bash
aws s3 mb s3://library-frontend-prod-<unique-suffix>
aws s3 sync dist/ s3://library-frontend-prod-<unique-suffix> --delete
```

Block public access ON; access only via CloudFront (recommended).

### 11. Create CloudFront distribution

- Origin: S3 bucket (OAC enabled)
- Default root object: `index.html`
- Custom error response: **403/404 → `/index.html`** (status 200) for React Router
- Alternate domain (optional): `app.yourdomain.com`
- SSL cert: **ACM** (must be in `us-east-1` for CloudFront)

---

## Phase 4 — Connect UI to API

### 12. CORS on backend

In `SecurityConfig`, add your CloudFront domain:

```java
config.setAllowedOrigins(List.of(
    "https://app.yourdomain.com",
    "https://<cloudfront-id>.cloudfront.net"
));
```

Rebuild and redeploy backend image.

### 13. API domain (recommended)

- Create subdomain `api.yourdomain.com`
- Route **53** alias → ALB
- Attach **ACM certificate** on ALB for HTTPS
- Rebuild frontend with:

```bash
VITE_API_URL=https://api.yourdomain.com
```

---

## Phase 5 — Security hardening

### 14. Security groups (minimum)

| From | To | Port |
|------|-----|------|
| Internet | ALB | 80/443 |
| ALB | ECS tasks | 8080 |
| ECS tasks | RDS | 3306 |
| CloudFront | S3 | HTTPS (OAC) |

Do **not** expose RDS to `0.0.0.0/0`.

### 15. Optional: AWS WAF

Attach WAF to ALB/CloudFront for rate limiting and bot protection (complements your app rate limit filter).

---

## Phase 6 — CI/CD (GitHub Actions → AWS)

### 16. Pipeline flow

1. On push to `main`:
   - `mvn verify` (backend)
   - `npm run build` (frontend with `VITE_API_URL`)
2. Build & push Docker image to **ECR**
3. Update **ECS service** (new task definition)
4. `aws s3 sync dist/` + **CloudFront invalidation** `/*`

Use OIDC role for GitHub → AWS (no long-lived AWS keys in repo).

---

## Phase 7 — Notifications (optional AWS-native)

Your app already has scheduled due/overdue logic. On AWS you can:

- **EventBridge** rule (daily cron) → trigger ECS task or Lambda  
- Or keep scheduler inside Spring Boot (simpler; already implemented)

For email/SMS:

- Integrate **Amazon SNS** from backend notification service (future enhancement)

---

## Phase 8 — Verify deployment

### 17. Smoke tests

1. `GET https://api.yourdomain.com/actuator/health` → `UP`  
2. Open `https://app.yourdomain.com`  
3. Register member → login → view books  
4. Librarian login → add book/author → issue/return book  
5. Check CloudWatch logs for errors  
6. Confirm RDS tables: `users`, `authors`, `books`, `members`, `lendings`, `notifications`

---

## Minimal “first deploy” order (checklist)

1. RDS MySQL + Secrets Manager  
2. ECR + ECS Fargate + ALB (backend)  
3. Health check green  
4. S3 + CloudFront (frontend)  
5. Set `VITE_API_URL` and rebuild frontend  
6. Update CORS + redeploy backend  
7. Route53 + ACM HTTPS  
8. CI/CD automation  

---

## Cost-saving dev environment

For a cheaper sandbox:

- RDS: `db.t3.micro`, single AZ  
- ECS: 1 task, 0.25 vCPU  
- CloudFront + S3 only (skip WAF initially)  
- No NAT Gateway if you accept public subnets for ECS (not ideal for prod)

---

## Common issues

| Issue | Fix |
|-------|-----|
| Register/login 400/401 | Check `VITE_API_URL`, CORS, JWT secret in ECS env |
| UI refresh 404 on `/books` | CloudFront SPA fallback to `index.html` |
| API cannot reach DB | RDS SG allows ECS SG on 3306; correct JDBC URL |
| Mixed content errors | Use HTTPS on both UI and API |

---

- `backend-task-definition.json` template  
- GitHub Actions workflow for ECR + ECS + S3 + CloudFront  
- sample `application-prod.yml` for RDS/Secrets Manager.

## Add author — request JSON

**Endpoint:** `POST /api/authors`  
**Role:** `LIBRARIAN` (JWT required)  
**Content-Type:** `application/json`

### Request body

```json
{
  "name": "George Orwell"
}
```

### Example cURL

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/authors \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <your-jwt-token>" \
  -d "{\"name\": \"George Orwell\"}"
```

### Example response (201 Created)

```json
{
  "authorId": 1,
  "name": "George Orwell",
  "linkedBooks": []
}
```

### Validation rules

| Field | Rule |
|--------|------|
| `name` | Required, not blank, max 100 characters |

### Update author (same JSON)

`PUT /api/authors/{id}` uses the same body:

```json
{
  "name": "George Orwell (Updated)"
}
```

input.description: Professional high-level system architecture diagram for a Library Management System. Clean white background, corporate tech style. Top: "Library Management System - High Level Architecture". Center flow left to right: Users/Browser box connects to Frontend Layer box containing React (Vite) UI, Nginx reverse proxy, JWT Auth. Frontend connects via REST API to Backend Layer box containing Spring Boot with modules: Auth & RBAC, REST Controllers, Business Services (Books, Authors, Members, Lendings, Notifications), Rate Limiting, Scheduler, JPA Repositories. Backend connects via JDBC to MySQL database cylinder with tables: users, authors, books, members, lendings, notifications. Bottom bar: Docker Compose, GitHub Actions CI, CloudWatch/Actuator monitoring. Use blue, green, purple accent colors, labeled arrows, rectangular boxes with rounded corners, suitable for documentation download.

input.description: Professional AWS cloud deployment architecture diagram for Library Management System. Orange AWS cloud boundary. Top: End Users. Left: Frontend Delivery with Amazon CloudFront CDN, Amazon S3 hosting React static build, AWS Certificate Manager for TLS. Center: Backend Compute with AWS WAF, Application Load Balancer, Amazon ECR, ECS Fargate running Spring Boot API with Auth JWT RBAC Books Authors Members Lendings Notifications, rate limiting, actuator health. Right: Data Security Ops with Amazon RDS MySQL library_db, AWS Secrets Manager, Amazon CloudWatch, Amazon SNS, EventBridge, IAM and KMS. Bottom: CI/CD GitHub Actions to ECR ECS S3 CloudFront. Bottom bar: app.yourdomain.com to CloudFront to S3, api.yourdomain.com to Route53 to ALB to ECS. Clean labeled arrows, corporate documentation style, white and light orange background, suitable for download.

content.text: [REDACTED]

The issue is that **`empty-window` is inside Cursor’s internal folder** (`C:\Users\atifk\.cursor\projects\`), not a normal project folder. Cursor often opens it as a chat/workspace shell, so you may see a prompt but **no file tree**.

Your actual code **is there** (`backend`, `frontend`, `README.md`, etc.), but you should open it from a normal location.

---

## Best fix: move project to a normal folder

### Step 1 — Copy the project (PowerShell)

```powershell
$src = "C:\Users\atifk\.cursor\projects\empty-window"
$dest = "C:\Users\atifk\projects\library-management-system"

New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dest

Copy-Item "$src\backend" "$dest\backend" -Recurse -Force
Copy-Item "$src\frontend" "$dest\frontend" -Recurse -Force
Copy-Item "$src\docs" "$dest\docs" -Recurse -Force
Copy-Item "$src\docker-compose.yml" "$dest" -Force
Copy-Item "$src\README.md" "$dest" -Force
Copy-Item "$src\.gitignore" "$dest" -Force
```

### Step 2 — Open in Cursor

1. **File → Open Folder…**
2. Select:
   ```
   C:\Users\atifk\projects\library-management-system
   ```
3. Click **Select Folder**

```
  frontend/
  docs/

  README.md
```
